title: CMD vs ENTRYPOINT
description:
## What Runs, and What Can Be Overridden?

A Dockerfile ends with:

```dockerfile
ENTRYPOINT ["python3", "app.py"]
CMD ["--port", "8080"]
```

The image is run as:

```bash
docker run myapp --port 9090
```

What command does the container actually execute?
explanation: `ENTRYPOINT` defines the fixed executable (`python3 app.py`) and `CMD` supplies **default arguments** (`--port 8080`). Any arguments you pass to `docker run` **replace** the `CMD` defaults but are appended to the `ENTRYPOINT`. So `docker run myapp --port 9090` runs `python3 app.py --port 9090`. (With only a `CMD` and no `ENTRYPOINT`, the run arguments would replace the whole command instead.)

hints.title: The rule of thumb
hints.body: ENTRYPOINT = the thing that always runs. CMD = default arguments that docker-run arguments override. They combine as ENTRYPOINT + (run args OR CMD).
